past_medical_history: Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 12 years ago, insulin-dependent
Aortic stenosis, moderate by last echocardiogram 18 months ago
Chronic kidney disease stage 3b (baseline creatinine 1.6 mg/dL)
family_history: Father died suddenly at age 58, cause unknown, suspected cardiac event
Mother had hypertension and type 2 diabetes, died at age 81 of stroke
Older brother had a pacemaker implanted at age 71 for slow heart rate

social_history.tobacco: Former smoker, 20-pack-year history, quit 18 years ago
social_history.alcohol: One to two beers on weekends
social_history.living_situation: Lives alone in a ground-floor apartment; daughter visits daily
social_history.exercise: Used to walk one mile daily until approximately 3 months ago when he stopped due to dizziness and fatigue

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert, oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Glasgow Coma Scale 15. Speech is fluent with normal rate and volume. No dysarthria or aphasia. Attention intact — able to perform serial sevens to 72. Recall 3/3 objects at 5 minutes. No confusion, no post-ictal features at time of examination.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II: Visual fields full to confrontation bilaterally. Pupils equal, round, reactive to light 3mm to 2mm bilaterally. No papilledema on fundoscopic exam. Mild arteriolar narrowing noted bilaterally consistent with chronic hypertension. CN III/IV/VI: Extraocular movements intact, no nystagmus. CN V: Sensation intact to light touch in V1-V3 bilaterally. Masseter strength symmetric. CN VII: Face symmetric at rest and with activation. CN VIII: Hearing intact to finger rub bilaterally. CN IX/X: Palate elevates symmetrically. CN XI: Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius 5/5 bilaterally. CN XII: Tongue midline without fasciculations or atrophy.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal throughout. Tone normal, no rigidity or spasticity. Strength 5/5 in all major muscle groups of upper and lower extremities bilaterally. No pronator drift.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch intact throughout. Pinprick mildly reduced in a stocking distribution below the ankles bilaterally, consistent with diabetic peripheral neuropathy. Vibration mildly diminished at bilateral great toes. Proprioception intact.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 2+ bilaterally, Achilles 1+ bilaterally. Plantar responses are flexor (downgoing) bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose normal bilaterally, no dysmetria. Heel-to-shin normal bilaterally. No intention tremor. Rapid alternating movements intact.
neurological_exam.gait: Steady with normal base. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: No tongue laceration. No urinary incontinence. No evidence of prolonged convulsive activity. Right parietal scalp contusion from fall, no active bleeding. Systolic crescendo-decrescendo murmur heard best at the right upper sternal border radiating to the carotids, consistent with known aortic stenosis.

patient.chief_complaint: Sudden loss of consciousness while walking to the bathroom
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Ryo Nakamura is a 82-year-old man with a significant cardiac history including moderate aortic stenosis, hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and chronic kidney disease who presents to the emergency department via ambulance after his daughter found him unresponsive on the hallway floor of his apartment at approximately 6:45 AM. According to the patient and his daughter, he had gotten out of bed and was walking to the bathroom when he suddenly felt a brief sensation of the room going dark, followed immediately by loss of consciousness. He fell forward, striking the right side of his head on the wall. His daughter, who had arrived early to check on him, heard the thud and found him lying on the floor. She estimates he was unresponsive for approximately 15 to 20 seconds. She reports that she did not witness any shaking, jerking, or stiffening of the limbs. There was no foaming at the mouth, no tongue biting, and no urinary or fecal incontinence. Upon regaining consciousness, Mr. Simmons was immediately lucid and coherent, asking his daughter why she was on the floor next to him. There was no period of confusion or disorientation. He reports a dull headache from striking the wall but denies any chest pain, shortness of breath, or palpitations before or after the event. Over the past two to three months, he has experienced four or five episodes of sudden lightheadedness lasting a few seconds each, sometimes accompanied by a sensation that his vision was graying out. These occurred at various times of day and in different positions, including while seated. He stopped his daily walks because of increasing fatigue and a fear of collapsing outside. He denies any prior seizures, and he has never had an episode of loss of consciousness before the recent cluster. He has not experienced any fever, weight loss, or night sweats. His daughter confirms he has been taking all his medications regularly and has not started any new drugs recently.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric, well-modulated, reactive to eye opening
background.sleep_features: Drowsiness captured with vertex waves and spindles; normal sleep architecture

artifacts.description: Intermittent electrode artifact from scalp contusion site, does not obscure underlying cerebral activity

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Performed for 3 minutes. Mild diffuse slowing during hyperventilation with prompt return to baseline. No epileptiform discharges provoked.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Performed at frequencies 1-30 Hz. Symmetric photic driving response. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Normal awake and drowsy EEG. No epileptiform discharges identified. No electrographic seizures captured during this 45-minute routine recording. This normal EEG does not support a diagnosis of epilepsy. Clinical correlation is recommended — cardiac monitoring should be pursued given the clinical presentation.
eeg.limitations: Routine 45-minute recording. A single normal interictal EEG does not exclude epilepsy; however, the clinical presentation is more consistent with cardiac syncope.
recommended_actions: Cardiac monitoring is the priority given the clinical scenario
If clinical suspicion for seizures persists despite negative cardiac workup, consider prolonged ambulatory EEG

ecg.rhythm: Complete heart block (third-degree AV block) with junctional/ventricular escape rhythm
findings: Complete AV dissociation with independent atrial rate of approximately 82 bpm
Slow ventricular escape rhythm at 32 bpm with wide QRS complex morphology
Left bundle branch block pattern in escape beats
Left axis deviation consistent with left anterior fascicular involvement
Prolonged QTc at 502 ms raising concern for torsades de pointes risk
Low-voltage QRS complexes in limb leads
No acute ST elevation to suggest STEMI
T-wave inversions in leads I and aVL, likely secondary to conduction abnormality
ecg.interpretation: Third-degree (complete) atrioventricular block with unreliable slow ventricular escape rhythm at 32 bpm. Complete AV dissociation confirmed by independent P waves marching through QRS complexes at a faster atrial rate. Widened QRS with LBBB morphology. Prolonged QTc at 502 ms, which in the setting of severe bradycardia poses a risk for torsades de pointes. This ECG represents a medical emergency requiring immediate cardiology consultation, continuous monitoring, and preparation for transcutaneous or transvenous pacing with subsequent permanent pacemaker implantation.
ecg.clinical_correlation: Complete heart block with extremely slow ventricular escape rhythm is the definitive explanation for this patient's syncope. The profound bradycardia leads to inadequate cardiac output and cerebral hypoperfusion, producing a classic Stokes-Adams attack. The known moderate aortic stenosis may exacerbate hemodynamic compromise at low heart rates. The prolonged QTc warrants vigilance for ventricular arrhythmias.

Mildly elevated, consistent with anemia of chronic disease

Mildly low, may reflect mild metabolic acidosis from CKD

Cardiac_markers.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated, likely demand-related in setting of severe bradycardia and aortic stenosis; serial monitoring recommended to exclude acute coronary syndrome
Significantly elevated, indicating cardiac dysfunction, likely multifactorial — aortic stenosis, chronic bradycardia, and CKD

Metabolic.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated despite allopurinol, consistent with CKD and history of gout
Normal thyroid function; hypothyroidism ruled out as contributor to bradycardia

labs.interpretation: Laboratory results demonstrate no acute metabolic derangement that would explain the syncopal episode. Electrolytes are largely normal with mild hyperkalemia likely related to CKD and losartan use. BUN and creatinine are elevated at the patient's CKD baseline. Mild normocytic anemia is present, consistent with chronic kidney disease. Troponin is mildly elevated, likely representing demand ischemia from profound bradycardia in the setting of aortic stenosis; serial troponins are recommended. BNP is significantly elevated, suggesting cardiac dysfunction from the combination of aortic stenosis and chronic bradycardia. TSH is normal, effectively excluding hypothyroidism as a cause of bradycardia. Glucose and HbA1c confirm suboptimally controlled diabetes.

Echocardiogram.clinical_significance: Moderately reduced systolic function, likely related to chronic pressure overload from aortic stenosis and prolonged bradycardia
Mildly dilated, consistent with chronic diastolic dysfunction from aortic stenosis
Moderately increased, consistent with left ventricular hypertrophy from aortic stenosis and hypertension
Moderate-to-severe aortic stenosis; progression from moderate 18 months ago. Mean gradient 38 mmHg, peak gradient 62 mmHg

Echocardiogram.value: Heavily calcified, restricted leaflet motion, moderate-to-severe stenosis
Echocardiogram.clinical_significance: Significant aortic stenosis with heavy calcification that may extend into the conduction system
Functional MR secondary to LV dysfunction

output.interpretation: Transthoracic echocardiogram demonstrates moderate-to-severe aortic stenosis with an aortic valve area of 0.9 cm2, representing progression from the moderate stenosis documented 18 months prior. Heavy aortic valve calcification is present. Left ventricular ejection fraction is moderately reduced at 40% with concentric left ventricular hypertrophy. Mild left atrial dilation. Mild functional mitral regurgitation. No pericardial effusion. The heavy calcification of the aortic valve likely extends into the adjacent conduction system, providing the pathophysiological basis for the complete heart block.
abnormal_values_summary: Left ventricular ejection fraction: 40% % - Moderately reduced systolic function, likely related to chronic pressure overload from aortic stenosis and prolonged bradycardia
Left atrial diameter: 4.1 cm - Mildly dilated, consistent with chronic diastolic dysfunction from aortic stenosis
Interventricular septum thickness: 1.4 cm - Moderately increased, consistent with left ventricular hypertrophy from aortic stenosis and hypertension
Aortic valve area: 0.9 cm2 - Moderate-to-severe aortic stenosis; progression from moderate 18 months ago. Mean gradient 38 mmHg, peak gradient 62 mmHg
Aortic valve: Heavily calcified, restricted leaflet motion, moderate-to-severe stenosis qualitative - Significant aortic stenosis with heavy calcification that may extend into the conduction system
Mitral valve: Mild regurgitation qualitative - Functional MR secondary to LV dysfunction

output.rhythm: Persistent complete heart block with ventricular escape rhythm throughout 24-hour recording
findings: Persistent complete AV block throughout entire 24-hour recording period
Average ventricular rate 34 bpm (range 26-40 bpm)
Atrial rate averaging 80 bpm, sinus rhythm throughout
Minimum ventricular rate 26 bpm at 02:18 AM with 5.1-second RR pause
Maximum ventricular rate 40 bpm during daytime activity
Two runs of non-sustained ventricular tachycardia (3-4 beats) at 04:12 AM and 05:33 AM
Isolated PVCs totaling 89 over 24 hours
QTc prolongation noted during bradycardic episodes, maximum 518 ms
output.interpretation: 24-hour Holter monitor confirms persistent complete (third-degree) AV block with unreliable, slow ventricular escape rhythm. Critically slow rates overnight with a maximum pause of 5.1 seconds, which carries risk of syncope and sudden cardiac death. Two runs of non-sustained ventricular tachycardia noted in the early morning hours, possibly facilitated by the prolonged QTc interval. This is a high-risk recording mandating urgent permanent pacemaker implantation.
output.clinical_correlation: The 5.1-second pause and runs of NSVT in the setting of prolonged QTc represent a substrate for both bradycardia-mediated syncope and potentially malignant ventricular arrhythmias (torsades de pointes). Permanent pacemaker implantation is urgently indicated. Given the reduced LVEF of 40%, a CRT-D (cardiac resynchronization therapy with defibrillator) should be discussed with electrophysiology.

Tilt_table_test.clinical_significance: Significant orthostatic drop secondary to inability to augment cardiac output due to complete heart block and aortic stenosis
No chronotropic response due to complete heart block
Symptoms reproduced rapidly due to hemodynamic compromise from fixed bradycardia and aortic stenosis
No vasovagal mechanism identified; hemodynamic compromise is entirely attributable to cardiac conduction disease and valvular heart disease

output.interpretation: Tilt table test does not demonstrate a vasovagal mechanism. The pronounced orthostatic hypotension and rapid symptom reproduction are attributable to the patient's complete heart block with fixed low ventricular rate, compounded by moderate-to-severe aortic stenosis limiting cardiac output augmentation. Vasovagal syncope is effectively excluded. The primary etiology of syncope is cardiac.
abnormal_values_summary: Baseline supine HR: 33 bpm - Reflects underlying complete heart block
70-degree tilt BP at 3 min: 82/48 mmHg - Significant orthostatic drop secondary to inability to augment cardiac output due to complete heart block and aortic stenosis
70-degree tilt HR at 3 min: 34 bpm - No chronotropic response due to complete heart block
Symptoms during tilt: Near-syncope at 2.5 minutes, test aborted qualitative - Symptoms reproduced rapidly due to hemodynamic compromise from fixed bradycardia and aortic stenosis

output.rhythm: Persistent complete heart block; ventricular escape rate did not increase with exercise
findings: Test terminated early at Stage 1 of Bruce protocol after 1 minute 50 seconds due to symptomatic hypotension, presyncope, and exertional dyspnea
Peak ventricular rate 36 bpm (complete chronotropic incompetence)
Sinus atrial rate increased to 118 bpm but with persistent AV dissociation
Blood pressure fell from 104/60 to 78/46 at peak exercise
2 mm ST depression in leads V4-V6 at peak exercise suggesting demand ischemia
No exercise-induced sustained ventricular arrhythmias
Patient reported chest tightness and severe lightheadedness prompting test termination
output.interpretation: Severely limited exercise stress test terminated early due to symptomatic hypotension and presyncope. Complete heart block prevented any chronotropic response to exercise. The combination of complete heart block and moderate-to-severe aortic stenosis produced profound hemodynamic compromise with minimal exertion. ST depression at peak exercise is consistent with demand ischemia in the setting of severe bradycardia and aortic stenosis rather than primary coronary lesion.
output.clinical_correlation: The inability to augment heart rate during exercise combined with fixed aortic obstruction confirms the hemodynamic severity of this patient's condition. Permanent pacemaker implantation is urgently required. Additionally, the aortic stenosis has progressed and surgical or transcatheter aortic valve replacement should be evaluated.

findings.type: Aortic valve calcification with conduction system extension
findings.location: Aortic annulus extending into the basal interventricular septum
findings.size: Extensive calcification spanning approximately 2.5 cm along the basal septum

signal_characteristics.T1: Signal void from dense calcification at the aortic annulus
signal_characteristics.late_gadolinium_enhancement: Mid-wall and subendocardial enhancement in the basal interventricular septum adjacent to the calcified aortic annulus, consistent with fibrosis from calcific extension into the conduction system

signal_characteristics.T1: Mildly elevated native T1 values globally, consistent with diffuse fibrosis from chronic pressure overload
signal_characteristics.late_gadolinium_enhancement: Patchy mid-wall enhancement in the basal and mid septum

additional_observations: Heavily calcified tricuspid aortic valve with severely restricted leaflet motion
No evidence of cardiac amyloidosis, sarcoidosis, or ARVC
output.impression: Cardiac MRI demonstrates extensive calcification of the aortic valve annulus extending into the basal interventricular septum, involving the region of the bundle of His and proximal bundle branches. This calcific extension provides a clear structural explanation for the complete heart block (calcific aortic stenosis-related conduction disease). Concentric LVH with diffuse interstitial fibrosis from chronic pressure overload is present. LVEF moderately reduced at 40%. No evidence of infiltrative cardiomyopathy.
recommended_actions: Permanent pacemaker implantation for symptomatic complete heart block
Cardiology evaluation for aortic valve intervention (TAVR or SAVR) given progressive moderate-to-severe aortic stenosis with reduced LVEF
Consider CRT-D given LVEF 40% and anticipated high ventricular pacing burden

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Calcific aortic stenosis with conduction system extension
differential_by_imaging.rationale: Extensive aortic annular calcification extending into the basal septum in the region of the conduction system, with fibrosis on LGE, in a patient with progressive aortic stenosis
Concentric LVH and diffuse interstitial fibrosis consistent with chronic hypertensive heart disease

CT_Head_Report.clinical_significance: Chronic small vessel ischemic disease, consistent with patient's age and cardiovascular risk factors

CT_Head_Report.value: Right parietal subgaleal soft tissue swelling without underlying fracture
CT_Head_Report.clinical_significance: Consistent with reported fall and scalp contusion, no fracture identified

output.interpretation: Non-contrast CT head shows no acute intracranial pathology. No intracranial hemorrhage, mass, midline shift, or acute ischemic changes. Moderate chronic small vessel ischemic disease appropriate for the patient's age and extensive cardiovascular risk factors. Right parietal scalp soft tissue swelling correlates with the reported fall. No skull fracture. An intracranial cause for the syncopal episode is effectively ruled out.
abnormal_values_summary: Calvarium: Right parietal subgaleal soft tissue swelling without underlying fracture qualitative - Consistent with reported fall and scalp contusion, no fracture identified

findings.location: Periventricular and subcortical white matter, bilateral

additional_observations: No acute infarction on DWI/ADC sequences
Normal ventricular system size and configuration
Mild generalized cortical volume loss appropriate for age
Right parietal scalp soft tissue swelling consistent with recent trauma
output.impression: Brain MRI demonstrates moderate chronic small vessel ischemic disease (Fazekas grade 2) without acute pathology. No acute infarction, hemorrhage, mass, or demyelinating disease identified. The brain MRI does not reveal a neurological explanation for the patient's syncope. Cardiac etiology should remain the focus of evaluation.
recommended_actions: No neuroimaging follow-up required for the syncopal episode
Focus evaluation on cardiac conduction disease

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Cardiac syncope due to complete (third-degree) atrioventricular block secondary to calcific aortic stenosis with conduction system involvement

differential.key_distinguishing: No witnessed tonic-clonic movements, no post-ictal confusion, no tongue biting, no urinary incontinence, immediate return to full alertness, normal EEG.
No identifiable vasovagal trigger, minimal prodromal warning, age and extensive cardiac history strongly favor cardiac etiology. Episodes also occur while seated.
While tamsulosin can contribute to orthostatic hypotension, the patient's prior episodes also occurred while seated. The complete heart block on ECG provides a definitive alternative explanation.

differential.diagnosis: Aortic stenosis-related syncope without conduction disease
differential.key_distinguishing: Moderate-to-severe aortic stenosis can independently cause exertional syncope, but the documented complete heart block is the primary mechanism. Aortic stenosis exacerbates the hemodynamic compromise.
No focal neurological deficits, TIA rarely causes true loss of consciousness, completely normal neurological exam, normal brain imaging.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain 12-lead ECG immediately upon emergency department arrival
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Complete heart block with ventricular escape rhythm at 32 bpm

optimal_actions.action: Initiate continuous cardiac monitoring via telemetry and establish IV access
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Persistent bradycardia with complete AV dissociation

optimal_actions.action: Apply transcutaneous pacing pads and have atropine and isoproterenol available at bedside
Obtain comprehensive laboratory studies including CBC, BMP, troponin, BNP, coagulation panel, TSH, and magnesium
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No metabolic cause for syncope; troponin mildly elevated (demand), BNP significantly elevated, mild anemia and CKD at baseline

optimal_actions.action: Urgent cardiology consultation for temporary transvenous pacing and permanent pacemaker planning
Obtain EEG to rule out epileptiform abnormalities as the cause of transient loss of consciousness
Obtain transthoracic echocardiogram to assess aortic stenosis severity and left ventricular function
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Moderate-to-severe aortic stenosis with LVEF 40%, LV hypertrophy, heavy aortic calcification

optimal_actions.action: Review and adjust medications — hold tamsulosin (may worsen hypotension), ensure no additional AV-nodal blocking agents are being administered
Admit to cardiac care unit for monitoring pending pacemaker implantation
Plan for permanent pacemaker implantation; discuss CRT-D given reduced LVEF and anticipated high pacing burden. Initiate referral for aortic valve intervention evaluation.

critical_actions: Obtain 12-lead ECG immediately to identify complete heart block
Prepare for transcutaneous or transvenous pacing
Urgent cardiology consultation for pacemaker evaluation
Hold tamsulosin and any agents that may worsen hypotension or AV conduction
Recognize that the syncope is cardiac in origin, not epileptic
Obtain serial troponins to evaluate for demand ischemia versus acute coronary syndrome
contraindicated_actions: Initiating antiepileptic drugs based on misdiagnosis of syncope as seizure
Administering beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, or any AV-nodal blocking agents
Discharging patient without cardiac monitoring and pacemaker evaluation
Administering IV adenosine (would worsen AV block and potentially cause asystole)
Performing lumbar puncture (no indication and would delay cardiac management)
Starting anticoagulation for suspected TIA (no evidence of cerebrovascular event)
Using metformin without reassessing renal function given potential for contrast studies
key_reasoning_points: Sudden, unheralded loss of consciousness without prodrome or warning is the hallmark of cardiac syncope (Stokes-Adams attack), distinguishing it from vasovagal syncope which typically has a prodrome
Absence of tonic-clonic activity, post-ictal confusion, tongue biting, and urinary incontinence argues strongly against epileptic seizure
Immediate return to full alertness after the event is characteristic of cardiac syncope and atypical for seizure, which usually produces a post-ictal period
ECG showing complete heart block with slow ventricular escape at 32 bpm provides the definitive diagnosis and mechanistic explanation for syncope

The known and progressive aortic stenosis with heavy calcification provides the pathophysiological substrate — calcific extension from the aortic annulus into the adjacent conduction system is a well-recognized cause of complete heart block in the elderly
Prolonged QTc at 502 ms in the setting of severe bradycardia creates additional risk for torsades de pointes, adding urgency to pacing
The normal EEG confirms the non-epileptic nature of the transient loss of consciousness
BNP elevation at 412 pg/mL reflects the hemodynamic burden of combined aortic stenosis and chronic bradycardia on the failing ventricle
Family history of a brother with pacemaker for bradycardia may suggest a genetic predisposition to conduction system disease

clinical_pearls: Calcific aortic stenosis is a common cause of acquired complete heart block in the elderly due to calcium extension from the aortic annulus into the adjacent His bundle and bundle branches
Cardiac syncope is characterized by sudden onset without prodrome and rapid full recovery without post-ictal confusion
Complete heart block is a Class I indication for permanent pacemaker implantation regardless of symptoms
Syncope in the setting of moderate-to-severe aortic stenosis is a Class I indication for aortic valve intervention
Prolonged QTc in the setting of bradycardia increases the risk of pause-dependent torsades de pointes
